original_abstract:
Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) is an essential tool in both neuroscience research and clinical practice for investigating the human brain in vivo. Despite the availability of various post-processing software packages, achieving fully automated end-to-end processing of dMRI datasets remains a significant technical challenge. To address this, we developed PANDA (Pipeline for Analyzing braiN Diffusion imAges), a MATLAB-based toolbox designed for the seamless, automated analysis of diffusion MRI data.

PANDA integrates established processing modules from a variety of reputable packages, including the FMRIB Software Library (FSL), the Pipeline System for Octave and Matlab (PSOM), Diffusion Toolkit, and MRIcron. The pipeline can ingest raw dMRI data in either DICOM or NIfTI format and automatically execute a comprehensive workflow—from initial preprocessing to the calculation of diffusion metrics such as fractional anisotropy (FA) and mean diffusivity (MD). These outputs are formatted for immediate statistical mapping at the voxel level, atlas-based region-of-interest level, and through Tract-Based Spatial Statistics (TBSS). Furthermore, PANDA automates the construction of whole-brain anatomical networks (the structural connectome) for all subjects.

A key feature of PANDA is its ability to handle large-scale datasets efficiently through parallel computing. It can distribute tasks across multiple cores on a single workstation or within a distributed environment (such as a Sun Grid Engine), significantly reducing processing time. Additionally, the toolbox features an intuitive graphical user interface (GUI) that allows researchers to easily configure input/output settings and fine-tune processing parameters. As an open-source resource, PANDA is freely available at http://www.nitrc.org/projects/panda/. We expect this toolbox to substantially lower the barrier to complex dMRI analysis and accelerate research into the human structural connectome.
humanized_abstract:
Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) has become a fundamental tool in both research and clinic for in vivo brain study. While many post-processing techniques and software packages have been developed, fully automatic end-to-end processing of dMRI data is still an open problem. In this paper, we proposed a MATLAB-based toolbox named PANDA (Pipeline for Analyzing braiN Diffusion imAges) for the fully automatic analysis of diffusion MRI data.

PANDA makes use of established processing modules from a range of open source image analysis software such as the FMRIB Software Library (FSL), the Pipeline System for Octave and Matlab (PSOM), Diffusion Toolkit and MRIcron. The pipeline is capable of accepting diffuson MR images (dMRI) as either DICOM or NIfTI files and will proceed with the tasks in a complete processing workflow. The workflow covers all steps from the initial processing of the diffusion data and estimation of fractional anisotropy (FA) and mean diffusivity (MD) diffusion metrics and that will allow for direct statistical analysis at voxel level, region level (using the atlas) as well as following the Tract-Based Spatial Statistics (TBSS) protocol. PANDA also streamlines the production of whole-brain anatomical brain networks (the structural connectome) for all subject datasets.

PANDA has the ability to deal with a large number of samples via parallel computing. With PANDA, each processing core on a local PC or in a cluster supported by Sun Grid Engine can work independently to greatly save time. A user-friendly GUI is provided for adjustments of input/output parameters and computation settings. PANDA is an open accessible toolbox and can be download from http://www.nitrc.org/projects/panda/. With PANDA, researchers can simply spend less time dealing with computation and pay more efforts to their brain connectome studies.

document.input:
Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) is an essential tool in both neuroscience research and clinical practice for investigating the human brain in vivo.
